data.title: AAII Sentiment Survey: Pessimism Hesitates
data.description:
Pessimism among individual investors about the short-term outlook for stocks decreased in the latest AAII Sentiment Survey. Meanwhile, optimism increased slightly, and neutral sentiment decreased.

Bullish sentiment, expectations that stock prices will rise over the next six months, increased 0.1 percentage points to 41.7%. Bullish sentiment is above its historical average of 37.5% for the second time in eight weeks.

Neutral sentiment, expectations that stock prices will stay essentially unchanged over the next six months, increased 3.1 percentage points to 19.1%. Neutral sentiment is unusually low and is below its historical average of 31.5% for the 62nd time in 64 weeks.

Bearish sentiment, expectations that stock prices will fall over the next six months, decreased 3.2 percentage points to 39.2%. Bearish sentiment is above its historical average of 31.0% for the 43rd time in 45 weeks.

The bull-bear spread (bullish minus bearish sentiment) increased 3.2 percentage points to 2.5%. The bull-bear spread is below its historical average of 6.5% for the 32nd time in 34 weeks.

This week’s special question asked AAII members what they think about the Federal Reserve’s decision to cut interest rates by 0.25 percentage points.

Here is how they responded:

  • It was the right move: 49.8%.
  • They should have cut rates by more percentage points: 27.0%.
  • They should have left it unchanged: 17.5%.
  • They should have raised rates: 2.1%.
  • Not sure/no opinion: 3.2%.

This week’s Sentiment Survey results:

  • Bullish: 41.7%, up 0.1 percentage points.
  • Neutral: 19.1%, up 3.1 percentage points.
  • Bearish: 39.2%, down 3.2 percentage points.

Historical averages:

  • Bullish: 37.5%.
  • Neutral: 31.5%.
  • Bearish: 31.0%.

The AAII Sentiment Survey has been conducted weekly since July 1987.

data.title: Harmony Biosciences: Cannabinoid Fragile X Fail Merits Rating Downgrade
data.description:
Investment Overview

Just over a month ago I awarded stock of Harmony Biosciences Holdings, Inc. (NASDAQ:HRMY) a Buy Rating - my second in succession, in fact, as I gave the Pennsylvania-based pharma a Buy rating in January this year, also.

Neither call has paid off so far, with Harmony shares down 30% since my first note, and 27% since my second. Most of these losses were recorded yesterday, as the marketer and seller of Wakix (pitolisant), approved to treat excessive daytime sleepiness (EDS) in adult patients with narcolepsy, reported a pipeline set-back.

Harmony provided an update from its Phase 3 Reconnect study of candidate ZYN002, the mechanism of action of which it describes as follows:

ZYN002 is the first-and-only pharmaceutically manufactured synthetic cannabidiol devoid of THC and formulated as a patent-protected permeation-enhanced gel for transdermal delivery through the skin and into the circulatory system.

The indication was Fragile X syndrome which Harmony discusses as follows:

Fragile X syndrome (FXS) is a rare genetic disorder that is the leading known cause of both inherited intellectual disability and autism spectrum disorder. The disorder negatively affects synaptic function, plasticity and neuronal connections, and results in a spectrum of intellectual disabilities and behavioral symptoms, such as social avoidance and irritability.

While the exact prevalence is unknown, upwards of 80,000 patients in the US and 121,000 patients in the European Union and the UK are believed to have FXS, based on FXS prevalence estimates of approximately 1 in 4,000 to 7,000 in males and approximately 1 in 8,000 to 11,000 in females. There is a significant unmet medical need in patients living with FXS as there are currently no FDA-approved treatments for this disorder.

The press release suggested that the Reconnect study did not meet the primary endpoint of improvement in social avoidance primarily due to a higher than expected placebo response rate, i.e., patients not receiving Harmony's drug performed unexpectedly well.

Analysis - A Not Unexpected Failure Perhaps

Harmony acquired ZYN002 via its acquisition of Zynerba Pharmaceuticals in 2023, in a deal worth ~$60m, with an additional $140m of contingent value rights ("CVRs") that paid out if the drug met certain study and regulatory milestones.

The drug had in fact already failed a clinical study, According to a Zynerba SEC filing:

In June 2020, we announced that Zygel did not achieve statistical significance versus placebo in the primary endpoint of improvement in the Social Avoidance subscale of the ABC-CFXS. Zygel also did not demonstrate statistical significance versus placebo in the three key secondary endpoints, which were the change from baseline to the end of the treatment period in the Irritability subscale score of the ABC-CFXS, the Socially Unresponsive/Lethargic subscale score of the ABC-CFXS and CGI-I.

In its press release announcing the acquisition Harmony had noted that:

Zygel is manufactured through a synthetic process in a cGMP facility and is not extracted from the cannabis plant. Therefore, it is devoid of THC, which is what causes the euphoric effect of cannabis, and has the potential to be a non-scheduled product if approved.

And also stated that:

Zygel showed positive signals in an open label Phase 2 trial in patients living with 22q11.2 deletion syndrome (22q), called the INSPIRE Trial.

The only approved cannabinoid product I am aware of that has secured an FDA approval is Jazz Pharmaceuticals' (JAZZ) Epidiolex, approved (since 2018) to treat Seizures associated with Lennox-Gastaut Syndrome, Dravet syndrome, and (since 2020) Tuberous Sclerosis Complex. The med earned $252m in Q2 2025.

In summary, then, Harmony may have been aware that a successful Phase 3 study readout was a long-shot, but concluded that $60m upfront was a worthwhile sum to pay in case the drug exceeded expectations - the CVRs will now no longer pay out as they were dependent on a positive Phase 3 readout.

During a recent fireside chat at the Cantor Global Healthcare Conference Harmony's CEO Jeffrey Dayno told the audience, explaining the mechanism of action ("MoA") of ZYN002:

Fragile X is a defect in the FMR1 gene. And that defect causes a lack of production of FMR protein. So with regards to the effect on that is disruption of the endocannabinoid system in the brain. So ZYN002 works at CB1 receptors in the brain to sort of generate homeostasis and of the endocannabinoid system. And disruption in that system is what causes the neurobehavioral symptoms.

So the mechanistic fit is explained by the underlying pathophysiology of disruption of the endocannabinoid system and ZYN002, especially through transdermal delivery, which causes -- results in steady-state plasma levels helps to generate homeostasis and regulate that system.

The CEO also compared ZYN002 favorably with Epidiolex, noting that it contained no THC (the part of cannabis that induces a euphoric state), "which is not good for kids with Fragile X," and suggested that:

So with positive data, then we're preparing to initiate a Phase III trial as well in 22q deletion syndrome. Both of these programs, we've had interactions with both FDA and EMA. And the importance of that is we have global rights to ZYN002. So with positive data and if we're successful, then we plan not only to file an NDA with the FDA, but we'll also, in parallel, work on preparing an MAA to submit to the European agency.

In fact, the CEO, prompted by the interviewer, went into a lot of detail around the patient population, the positive results from the Phase 2 Connect study in a subset of patients with complete methylation in the FMR1 gene, and the way the Reconnect study has been engineered to limit the placebo response, but ultimately, as we now know, to no avail.

Despite blaming a higher than expected placebo response, Harmony's press release doesn't provide any information on plans to continue developing the therapy, which may be interpreted as a sign the company will shelve the program entirely. Instead, the CEO was quoted in the press release stating:

We have a late-stage, catalyst-rich pipeline with multiple Phase 3 programs in the clinic and continue to be on track to initiate our Phase 3 trials for pitolisant HD in narcolepsy and idiopathic hypersomnia in the fourth quarter of this year. Harmony’s unique profile as a profitable self-funding biotech company with strong cash-generation positions us well to drive future growth.

Looking Ahead - Are There Still Reasons To Be Positive About Harmony?

As I noted in my post in August:

In Q2, Harmony missed the Street's expectations on both earnings per share of $0.92 on an adjusted basis and $0.68 on a GAAP basis, and on revenues of $200.5m—up 16% year-on-year. Net income rose year-on-year from $11.6m in Q2 2024 to $39.8m.

These were narrow misses, however, which did not create a sell-off of stock. Management stated in its earnings press release that it remained on track for its forecast of $820m to $860m of revenues in 2025.

Across the first six months of 2025, revenues came to $385.2m—up 18% year-on-year—total operating expenses to $210.7m—up ~8%, net income to $85.4m, and EPS to $1.49, up >70% and 69%, respectively.

Harmony's current market cap is $1.53bn, which means its forward price to sales ratio is ~1.8x, and its forward price to earnings, if we multiply 1H earnings per share by 2 to get an estimated full-year figure of $2.98, ~9x. I pointed out in my last post that by P/S and P/E ratios Harmony stock looks cheap, and after this week's sell-off that is even more so the case.

As we can see above, even without ZYN002 Harmony's pipeline still looks quite rich.

For example, Phase 3 data from candidate EPX-100, a 5-HT2 (serotonin) agonist, in patients with Dravet Syndrome and Lennox-Gastaut syndrome, is due next year, although yesterday's news is not the first setback the company has faced this year.

In Q1, the FDA rejected the company's supplemental new drug application ("sNDA") for pitolisant for the treatment of excessive daytime sleepiness (EDS) in adult patients with idiopathic hypersomnia ("IH"). In a press release Harmony commented:

We made the decision to submit the sNDA based on our belief in pitolisant’s overall benefit-risk profile in IH and our deep commitment to the patient community, despite the challenge associated with this submission.

Our long-term business strategy has always been to extend our leadership in sleep/wake through the development of Pitolisant HD, an enhanced and higher-dose formulation, which represents the more significant opportunity in IH given its optimized profile and provisional patent filed out to 2044.

We are on track to initiate a Phase 3 registrational trial of Pitolisant HD in IH in Q4 2025, with a target PDUFA date in 2028.

Opportunities for pitolisant in Prader Will Syndrome, and myotonic dystrophy intrigue, especially given the current state of play in PWS, and meanwhile, Harmony is advancing its Orexin 2 Receptor (OX2R) Agonist candidate BP1.15205, which could supplant pitolisant as the standard of care in sleep/wake disorders long-term, management hopes.

Concluding Thoughts - After A Tough 2025, Am I Still Bullish On Harmony?

As of the end of Q2, Harmony reported $672.3m of cash, and as I have already mentioned, I'd typically expect a pharma guiding for >$800m of full-year revenues, and profits, to trade substantially higher than $1.5bn, especially one showing double-digit year-on-year revenues growth.

If approvals for a higher dose pitolisant can be secured, patent protections could be extended and revenues might increase significantly, and these catalysts are very much in play, plus there is pivotal PWS data arriving next year, so in may ways I'd like to uphold my buy rating and advise investors to buy the dip.

Ultimately, however, I'll take a more cautious approach and downgrade stock to hold. I think the rejection of its sNDA and the ZYN002 study fail are somewhat problematic for Harmony, especially given that, regarding the latter, management had stated it had a "high degree of confidence and conviction in the success of the Phase III registrational trial."

Although Harmony has blamed placebo performance for the fail, the fact it has not outlined any plans to keep developing the drug hints at another story potentially being told - one of over-promising and under-delivering.

If that can happen with ZYN002, perhaps it will happen with pitolisant HD, and pitolisant in PWS also, and perhaps the orexin agonist has been a little over-hyped also.

That may be a little unfair on management and as mentioned, Harmony stock feels cheap, however the business is heavily dependent on Wakix revenues continuing to grow, and I note that there are two major rivals in its indication - Jazz Pharmaceuticals (JAZZ) Xyway, and Avadel's (AVDL) Lumryz, and that Xyway is now subject to cheaper generic competition. Furthermore, Harmony notes in its 2024 annual report:

Other potential future competitive products are currently in development, including Axsome Therapeutics’ (AXSM) AXS-12 (reboxetine) product candidate, Suven Life Sciences’ SUVN-G3031 H3 inverse agonist product candidate, NLS Pharmaceutics (NLSP) mazindol ER product candidate and orexin 2 receptor agonist product candidates from Takeda (TAK), Jazz Pharmaceuticals/Sumitomo Pharma, Centessa and Alkermes among others.

For a company with a single commercial product, to have such a high level of competitive threat facing that product is also troubling, in my view, and therefore I am stepping on to the sidelines, and intend to keep an eye on Harmony's progress from afar, for the time being.

data.title: Williams-Sonoma: Strong Execution Amid Potential Housing Rebound
data.description:
The stock market is showing signs of strain near all-time highs, as Fed Chair Powell's comments that stocks look expensive are driving a loss of faith in the tech trade that has dominated the market this year. In my view, this is a great time for investors to rotate our portfolios out of momentum-driven large caps and into catalyst-driven small- and mid-cap stocks.

In my view, Williams-Sonoma, Inc. (NYSE:WSM) is a good "Buy." The leading furniture provider in the United States stands to benefit from a potential rebound in the housing market, driven by interest rate declines. The stock is only about ~flat this year, and with the stock trading at reasonable valuations, there's still room on the upside.

I'm initiating Williams-Sonoma at a "Buy" rating.

Williams-Sonoma's position in the furniture industry

Let's first start with Williams-Sonoma's standing in the broad and highly fragmented furniture industry. Williams-Sonoma defines itself as a specialty home furniture company, and it's the largest in its category, with FY24 revenue of $7.7 billion dwarfing its next-largest peer RH (RH) by more than 2x.

The company has four principal brands: Pottery Barn, which is its largest contributor at ~$3 billion, or 43% of its revenue, followed by West Elm, the titular Williams-Sonoma, and Pottery Barn Kids & Teen. The company also has a portfolio of other brands that comprise roughly 5% of revenue, which include lighting company Heirloom, personalized gift company Mark & Graham, and upscale furniture label GreenRow. Roughly two-thirds of the company's sales are via e-commerce now, and one-third are through the company's physical store footprint.

Like many other furniture players, Williams-Sonoma has been struggling with weaker post-COVID demand. The company's revenue peaked at $8.7 billion in FY22. That said, the company is on the way back up. Its guidance this year calls for 2%-5% y/y comp sales growth and 0.5%-3.5% y/y overall growth (against a tough 53-week fiscal year comparison in FY24).

Stronger execution amid a flatlining industry

The furniture industry has had no shortage of difficulties this year, dealing with the fallout of lower demand as well as the pressure of tariffs. But amid these challenges, Williams-Sonoma has executed tremendously on both the top and bottom line.

As shown in the chart above, in Williams-Sonoma's most recent quarter, the company hit 2.7% y/y revenue growth, with comparable brand sales growth (excluding the impact of newly opened stores) growing at a faster 3.7% y/y clip. Overall sales growth in the first half of FY25 hit 3.5% y/y, which importantly eclipsed rival Wayfair Inc.'s (W) sales growth of 2.7% y/y in the same period.

Importantly, Williams-Sonoma's management has noted that the company is gaining market share, as the broader furniture category as a whole declined during this time. The company notes that product newness is driving shopper engagement. The company is also building on its B2B business initiatives, supplementing more tepid customer demand with direct furniture sales to businesses. Per CEO Laura Alber's remarks on the Q2 earnings call:

This was our second quarter of accelerated positive comps coming out of 2024 despite continued geopolitical uncertainty and no material improvement in the housing market. And we continue to outperform the industry, which declined in Q2. This growing outperformance was driven by positive comps in both furniture and non-furniture and strong performance in our retail and e-commerce channels [...]

Also, our B2B business remains an important part of our growth strategy. B2B grew 10% in Q2 with both trade and contract performing. We continue to build a loyal expanding client base across multiple industries by leveraging our design expertise and commercial-grade product range. Our emerging brands, including Rejuvenation, Mark and Graham and GreenRow, together also continue to grow double-digits.

Before strong Q2 results rolled in, Williams-Sonoma was expecting just 0%-3% comps growth; now, it's expecting 2 points stronger growth, at 2%-5% y/y.

What I find more compelling about Williams-Sonoma beyond the improved growth prospects, however, is its deft management of tariffs and a rising cost environment. In Q2, Williams-Sonoma's gross margins rose 220bps y/y to 47.1%. The company has six different strategies for reducing its tariff exposure, including negotiating better prices with suppliers, shifting country of origin, and selectively increasing end-customer prices.

We note that Williams-Sonoma's gross margin dwarfs that of Wayfair, where margins of 30.1% in Q2 slid -10 bps y/y. We note as well that Williams-Sonoma is roughly two points ahead of the ~45% margins for direct competitor Restoration Hardware:

In my view, Williams-Sonoma's gross margin premium against its peers and its market share gains in a difficult time position the company well for a rebound if and when the housing market makes a rebound. In my view, moving is one of the key drivers behind new furniture purchases—which is what drove the demand burst in 2022 for Williams-Sonoma in the first place, when flexible work allowed families to leave cities searching for more space.

The combination of structurally higher gross margins versus peers, plus a potential leap in sales to generate economies of scale on opex, can drive meaningful EPS growth for Williams-Sonoma.

Valuation and key takeaways

Adding icing to the cake is the fact that Williams-Sonoma trades at a lower valuation versus its peers. Its 23x forward P/E ratio sits slightly below Restoration Hardware (which is admittedly growing faster than Williams-Sonoma but also sits at a slightly lower gross margin profile) and well below Wayfair.

Considering the fact that Williams-Sonoma achieved 20% EPS growth in its most recent quarter before any tailwinds from a housing rebound, I'd say that a low-20s P/E multiple is a great entry point for this company. Stay long here.

data.title: SPSM: Profitable Small Caps Left Behind, But They Still Look Attractive
data.description:
The Russell 2000 made headlines earlier this month for finally eclipsing its November 2021 all-time high. U.S. small-cap stocks have performed well leading up to and immediately following the Fed's resumption of its rate-cutting cycle. Interestingly, the higher-quality S&P SmallCap 600 has lagged—it's not a new phenomenon, however.

I had a "Buy" rating on the SPDR® Portfolio S&P 600™ Small Cap ETF (NYSEARCA:SPSM) a year ago. Shares are up 4% over that time but have badly lagged the S&P 500 and have trailed the iShares Russell 2000 ETF (IWM). Still, I see more upside ahead. The valuation is attractive, and investors could return to more profitable small caps as the cycle unfolds.

SPSM Losing Ground to IWM

According to the issuer, SPSM "seeks to provide investment results that, before fees and expenses, correspond generally to the total return performance of the S&P SmallCap 600 Index." It's a low-cost ETF that "seeks to offer precise, comprehensive exposure to small-cap US equities." "The index is float-adjusted and market capitalization-weighted." Keep in mind that the S&P SmallCap 600 index has a profitability screen that the Russell 2000 Index does not have.

SPSM is a large ETF with $12.7 billion in assets under management as of Sept. 24, 2025. That’s up from $11.7 billion at the time of my previous analysis. Its annual expense ratio is low at just three basis points, while the trailing 12-month dividend yield is only a bit above that of the S&P 500 at 1.74%.

Share-price momentum has been softening as the recovery off the post-Liberation Day bottom has played out, earning SPSM a medium C ETF Grade in that category by Seeking Alpha’s quantitative scoring system. The risk level is rated poorly, but I’d point out that the reason is a relatively high (not an absolutely high) standard deviation level. The volatility has not been all that intense in the last few months.

Finally, the liquidity situation is healthy. Average daily volume is high at 1.77 million shares, while the median 30-day bid/ask spread is just two basis points, per SSGA Funds.

For an update on the portfolio, the 3-star, Gold-rated ETF by Morningstar plots along the bottom row of the style box, indicating its high weight to domestic small caps. What helps from a diversification perspective is that there is almost an even balance among value, blend, and growth holdings.

As for the valuation, the price-to-earnings ratio remains low at just a smidgen above 16x. Couple that with a long-term EPS growth rate of 11.2%, and the resulting PEG ratio is compelling at 1.43x. That’s far below the S&P 500’s PEG ratio.

SPSM: Portfolio & Factor Profiles

SPSM’s sector breakdown is also well diversified. There’s a quartet of the biggest sector weights: Financials, Industrials, Information Technology, and Consumer Discretionary.

Each of those four commands a 14% to 19% weight in the ETF. That’s also a rounded-out mix of cyclical, value, and growth.

SPSM: Holdings & Dividend Information

Seasonally, investors should gird themselves for a potentially bullish period. Historically, mid-October through year-end has been favorable, with particularly strong returns occurring in November.

I’m encouraged by generally solid price action during what can be a precarious calendar stretch over the back half of the third quarter.

SPSM: Bullish Seasonality On Tap

The Technical Take

With the fund price well on an earnings basis, SPSM’s technical situation remains mixed. Notice in the chart below that, unlike the Russell 2000, the SmallCap 600 is materially below its late 2024 high. Moreover, there’s emerging resistance very close to the current level at $47. The long-term 200-day moving average, meanwhile, is trending downward during this broader bull market for global stocks.

Also take a look at the RSI momentum oscillator at the top of the graph. While the 200dma is not so sanguine, the momentum indicator looks good (as it’s above 40). But with a high amount of volume by price up to $47, the onus remains on the bulls to carry SPSM through noted resistance. I do like, though, that a bullish golden cross pattern occurred, in which the 50dma moved above the 200dma.

SPSM: $47 Resistance, Decent RSI Trends, Falling 200dma

The Bottom Line

I have a "Buy" rating on SPSM. The S&P SmallCap 600 ETF, which holds more profitable companies than the Russell 2000, has lagged the S&P 500 in the past year. Still, I like the valuation setup, while the technicals are not quite as bright.

data.title: Immuneering: Positive Pancreatic Cancer Data Atebimetinib Leads To Q4 2025 Catalyst
data.description:
The last time I wrote about Immuneering Corporation (NASDAQ:IMRX) it was in a Seeking Alpha article entitled "Immuneering: Phase 1 Data Release IMM-1-104 Leads To Additional 2024 Milestone." With respect to this article, I talked about positive phase 1/2a study data released using IMM-1-104 [now known as atebimetinib] for the treatment of patients with RAS mutant solid tumors. At that time, I had a "Buy" Rating on the stock, but as I will explain below, I believe that I should move the stock to a "Strong Buy" rating instead. It is because of the progress it has made to date, along with several key catalysts expected within the next 1-year period.

It recently released positive results from its phase 2a study using its lead drug atebimetinib [IMM-1-104] in combination with modified gemcitabine/nab-paclitaxel [mGnP] for the treatment of patients with 1st-line pancreatic cancer. As I will show below, this MEK inhibitor was able to substantially increase both overall survival [OS] and progression-free survival [PFS] in these patients with the latest data set. That is, this drug candidate was able to substantially exceed the current standard of care [SOC] with respect to these two efficacy measures.

If the company has already reported positive data from this phase 2a study, then why do I believe that there is still upside left here? It is because with this positive data on hand, it is ready to receive regulatory feedback from the FDA so that it can initiate a pivotal phase 3 study for this program. Such feedback is expected in Q4 of 2025 and would allow it to move forward.

With that being said, it believes that it could potentially initiate a pivotal phase 3 study using its MEK inhibitor atebimetinib in combination with mGnP by the end of 2025. From there, the first patient is expected to be dosed in mid-2026. This company is in excellent shape, because with the proof-of-concept it has been able to establish thus far, it is able to expand the use of atebimetinib towards other types of solid tumors. With that being said, it expects to initiate additional combination arms of this MEK inhibitor in targeting patients with various solid tumors. However, more importantly, during this year it has already been able to establish two clinical trial supply agreements with Eli Lilly (LLY) and Regeneron Pharmaceuticals (REGN) to explore the use of atebimetinib to treat patients with advanced solid tumors. The point is that this opens the door to expand to the large non-small cell lung cancer [NSCLC] market.

Continued Improvement Of Atebimetinib Treated Pancreatic Cancer Patients Bodes Well For Expected FDA Feedback

As I noted above, Immuneering Corporation was able to achieve positive results from its phase 2a study using its lead MEK inhibitor atebimetinib in combination with modified gemcitabine/nab-paclitaxel [mGnP] to treat 1st-line pancreatic cancer patients. These are not just any patients, though, the goal of the study was to go after RAS-mutated or RAS/MAPK pathway activated solid tumors. The reasoning for this is because the drug as a MEK inhibitor was developed to only specifically target advanced solid tumor patients with these pathway mutations. Before going over the positive phase 2a study in detail, plus any catalysts that are to be released specifically for this program, it is important to go over what this type of cancer is and what the possible market opportunity for the company it could end up being.

Pancreatic cancer, as the name suggests, is the growth of malignant cells on the pancreas to form a tumor. specifically, it starts in the ducts of the pancreas. What makes this type of cancer really bad is that first and foremost it is a very vital organ. It both helps to aid the body in terms of digestion and also releases hormones necessary to regulate blood sugar levels. Secondly, this type of cancer is very hard to detect because in the beginning there are hardly any symptoms at all. In turn, this cancer is not ultimately detected until the cancer has already reached late-stage disease and spread to other parts of a person's body. Again, symptoms come later when the cancer has already invaded other organs. Such possible symptoms that a patient with this disorder can experience are as follows:

  • Jaundice [yellowing of the skin]
  • Fatigue
  • Nausea
  • Blood clots forming
  • Back pain
  • Stomach pain.

The global pancreatic cancer market is expected to reach $9.57 billion by 2034. However, it is important to note that the company is only targeting RAS mutated pancreatic cancer patients. Despite the company only focusing on this subset of patients, it would still be a huge market opportunity anyways. What makes me believe this to be the case? That's because it is said that approximately 90% to 93% of pancreatic cancer patients have the RAS mutation [predominantly KRAS being the most prominent one for these patients].

In order to see if its MEK inhibitor atebimetinib would be capable of being able to help treat patients with 1st-line RAS-mutant metastatic or advanced pancreatic cancer, it is in the process of running the ongoing phase 2a study. It is important to note that this mid-stage trial was first sought out as the ability to evaluate the use of this drug either alone as a monotherapy or in combination with other agents specifically approved to treat RAS-mutant solid tumors. I point this out, because the company then set out to initiate specific cohorts to target a certain group of patients within this trial.

With that being said, it is in terms of a phase 2a study that explored the use of atebimetinib in combination with mGnP to treat 1st-line pancreatic cancer patients. As I noted in the beginning of this article, it released positive data from this mid-stage study, showing that this combination was able to significantly increase the rates of overall-survival [OS] and progression-free survival [PFS] over a 9-month period. That is, a total of 34 patients given atebimetinib + mGnP achieved the following in 9 months:

  • Overall-survival of 86% [median OS not being reached yet at time of data cut off]
  • Progression-free survival of PFS of 53%.

These efficacy numbers look good on their own without any context. But when you bring in the current standard of care [SOC] for these patients, which is gemcitabine/nab-paclitaxel, it is a huge difference. Patients who are given this SOC regimen achieve OS and PFS rates of 47% and 29% at 9 months, respectively. The selected dose of choice for this study was for these patients to receive 320 mg of once-daily oral atebimetinib plus intravenous [IV] infusion of mGnP. It was shown to not only achieve this desired efficacy I described above, but was also noted to be safe for patients as well. There were only two categories to make note of in terms of observing Grade 3 level of adverse events [AEs] and this would be with respect to neutropenia and anemia. There is no cause for concern with this, because typically these particular AEs are noted to be found for patients who take SOC chemotherapy to treat their pancreatic cancer disease.

Again, the data is out of the way, but this doesn't mean that there aren't some milestones left for investors to look forward to. The first and most important event relating to the development of atebimetinib plus mGnP would be the FDA regulatory feedback the company expects in Q4 of 2025. The importance of this is to gain input from the FDA on a potential trial design of a phase 3 study to advance this atebimetinib combination for these RAS-mutant 1st-line pancreatic cancer patients. If it gains insightful feedback from the FDA, then this sets up the next batch of two catalysts.

One expected catalyst would be the initiation of a phase 3 study using the atebimetinib combination to treat these patients by the end of 2025 and then the actual dosing of the first patient for this trial in mid-2026. There is also an expansion opportunity in line for this program, which goes back to what I stated above, which is that the phase 2 trial for the entire program started off as a basket trial targeting multiple solid tumor types.

With that being said, Immuneering intends to initiate other combination arms of atebimetinib targeting other solid tumor types in 2026. However, it is not yet known what types of solid tumors it will evaluate as part of this expansion.

A Double Win With Clinical Trial Supply Agreements Based On Positive Atebimetinib Drug Development

With the positive results observed to date in the ongoing phase 2a study using MEK inhibitor atebimetinib to treat patients with MAPK pathway driven solid tumors, there is one major expansion opportunity underway in the targeting of patients with non-small cell lung cancer [NSCLC]. Specifically, Immuneering has been able to establish two major clinical trial supply collaboration agreements to date in terms of this lead drug it is working on. I believe that it is important to highlight that each collaboration with a different pharmaceutical company is exploring a different type of drug to combine atebimetinib with.

The first of such collaboration agreements, announced earlier this year in February of 2025, was that established with Regeneron Pharmaceuticals. That is, this specific program is evaluating the use of atebimetinib in combination with PD-1 inhibitor Libtayo to treat unresectable or metastatic RAS-mutant NSCLC patients in a phase 2a study. The downside is that Immuneering has to continue to fund this particular study, but on the flip side Regeneron is going to provide the entire supply of Libtayo needed to run this study. The rationale for doing this agreement is that preclinical data stemming from 2022 noted that deep cyclic inhibition of MEK inhibitor atebimetinib was able to enhance the effect of checkpoint blockade therapies. In addition, to the combination potentially having a greater effect on breaking the cycle of MAPK pathway addiction to driving continued tumor growth.

The second of such collaboration agreements established this year was that of the company with Eli Lilly. The reason for this is so that atebimetinib as a dual MEK inhibitor could be combined with the big pharma's second-generation KRAS G12C inhibitor olomorasib [LY3537982] to target locally advanced or metastatic KRAS G12C-mutant non-small cell lung cancer [NSCLC]. This agreement is also going to be evaluated in a phase 2 study. The thing to note here is that this is going to target advanced cancer patients, that is those who had failed to respond to a prior systemic therapy. Just like the other deal noted above, the goal is to have Eli Lilly provide supply of olomorasib for this mid-stage trial. The bottom line is that with respect to both deals, Immuneering maintains full commercial rights to atebimetinib [IMM-1-104].

The goal of the company exploring combinations is to have a dual blockade of the RAS/MAPK pathway. This vertical blockade in using atebimetinib plus olomorasib has a dual effect on this entire pathway. In preclinical testing, such a combination was shown to achieve superior enhanced tumor regression and delayed tumor resistance as well. The point here is that sometimes the cancer adapts to the targeting of only one pathway. It is believed this dual MAPK pathway approach could potentially yield a superior outcome, instead of atebimetinib alone but this remains to be seen. The beauty of this company is that its drug atebimetinib offers deep cyclic inhibition in terms of its technology.

That is, it takes a "pulsating" targeting approach to shrinking or treating solid tumors. While it shrinks tumors in a slower fashion with this drug development method, one key item out of this is that the cancer can't resist or adapt because of this. In addition, this approach leads to restoration of full transient signaling to healthy cells, while killing the tumor site at the same time. The end result is a therapy that works effectively to kill of a tumor, but in a safer/tolerable fashion. Other cancer drugs have a sustained approach and while it works, a problem is that the tumor is able to adapt and gain resistance. In essence, tumors shrink fast, but gain the resistance I described above with a sustained targeting approach.

Financials

According to the 10-Q SEC Filing, Immuneering Corporation had cash and cash equivalents of $26.4 million as of June 30, 2025. The truth is that this was not a lot of cash on hand at all and this is based on what the company highlighted in its Q2 of 2025 financial results and earnings update. It noted that it believed its cash on hand would only be enough to fund its operations at least into 2026. The reason for the cash on hand is because the company tapped into an at-the-market [ATM] agreement it had made with Piper Sandler & Company. This is with respect to allowing it to sell from time to time as needed up to an aggregate of $50 million worth of its common shares of stock.

During the 6-months ending June 30, 2025 it raised approximately $13.7 million after expenses with the sale of 4,836,804 shares of its common stock at $2.95 per share. The cash projection was not ideal and thus that's why the company just enacted a cash raise.

There are two parts to this specific financial transaction. The first of which is in terms of a public offering of Class A common stock, which is expected to bring in $175 million. Then the other side of it is that there was a concurrent private placement agreement with Sanofi to purchase up to $25 million worth of Class A common stock. Both transactions are expected to close around the same time on September 26, 2025. The company burns about $14.8 million per quarter. This is broken down into $10.5 million in R&D expenses and then $4.3 million in G&A expenses.

Risks To Business

There are several risks that investors should be aware of before investing in Immuneering. The first risk to consider would be in terms of the positive 9-month data that was just achieved from the phase 2a study using atebimetinib plus mGnP to target patients with 1st-line pancreatic cancer. The company intends to receive regulatory feedback on a proposed phase 3 study design to use this atebimetinib combination to treat these patients. The risk here is that there is no assurance that this meeting outcome will end up being positive for starters. Nor that the company is going to receive a green light from the FDA to initiate a pivotal phase 3 study for this program.

The second risk to consider would be in terms of the ongoing collaboration agreements Immuneering had developed with Eli Lilly and Regeneron Pharmaceuticals. Even though atebimetinib has shown to be synergistic with PD-1 and KRAS G12C inhibitors in targeting solid tumors, there is no guarantee that upon the release of data from either of these phase 2 studies, these results will turn out to be positive. Nor that the data itself will be enough to warrant continuation of either program towards phase 3 clinical testing thereafter.

The third and final risk to consider would be in terms of additional combination arms the company is looking to explore. It thinks that in 2026 it will be able to evaluate the use of atebimetinib with other drugs against a variety of solid tumor types. The risk here is that even though the company has achieved remarkable results in using this MEK inhibitor to target patients with RAS-mutant/MAPK pathway pancreatic cancer patients, there is no assurance it will work just as well or better against other solid tumor types.

Conclusion

Immuneering has done well to get to where it is now in terms of pipeline advancement of its lead MEK inhibitor atebimetinib for the treatment of patients with 1st-line RAS-mutant pancreatic cancer. Specifically, the latest positive phase 2a data stems from the combination of this drug together with SOC mGnP to target these patients. Again, I believe that there is further value to be unlocked here because these positive results brings forth several catalysts to keep an eye on. The most important inflection point would be with the expected FDA meeting expected in Q4 of 2025. This is a key item to watch for, because successful talks with this U.S. regulatory agency would mean the ability for the company to initiate a phase 3 study using atebimetinib to treat these 1st-line pancreatic cancer patients.

As I have shown above, 320 mg oral dosing of this drug together with mGnP was able to allow the generation of superior OS and PFS in these patients compared to the third-party MPACT pivotal trial using SOC mGnP. It's not always ideal to make cross-trial comparisons, but even without this comparison, the data still speaks for itself in terms of what has been generated to date. With positive data achieved in the phase 2a study using atebimetiib plus mGnP in targeting 1st-line pancreatic cancer patients, plus the expected end-of-phase 2 [EOP2] guidance meeting in Q4 of 2025, I believe it is important to move this stock from a Buy rating to a Strong Buy.

data.title: United States Antimony: Valuation Has Become Stretched After DoD Deal Rally (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
United States Antimony (NYSE:UAMY) or "USAC" is pulling back after an extended rally, down about 20% from its Tuesday peak by Thursday morning before bouncing back up. UAMY is still up about 39% over the past month, gapping up earlier this week after it was awarded a sole-source, five-year DoD contract that may bring the firm $245M in sales, with orders starting this week.

My history with UAMY goes back to 2019, when it was worth only fifty cents. Six years later, my article "United States Antimony: 'Department Of Defense Put' To Rescue Share Prices" seems to be proven exactly correct, with UAMY over 10X higher today. At the time, I saw US Antimony's effective monopoly (or sole producer) on domestic antimony production as giving it a high probability of continued support from the DoD. Antimony is needed for weapons, flame retardants, and solar panels. Its weapons usage is critical given that China dominates global exports, restricting these sales to the US about a year ago, causing the element to become far more expensive.

I covered the stock last in the July article, "United States Antimony: Bull Run May Continue If Mining Ability Proven By September." Well, September's here, and UAMY is ~140% higher. My bullish outlook was mainly based on the potential value of its Alaska mining project. There has been some news on this project, with the company recently reporting cleaning up land for use and hosting a "VIP site visit" on its property as it looks to build relations with local regulators. After that event, the company started reclamation operations to extract antimony from local discarded rock waste at former mining sites.

USAC's Valuation Faces Execution Risk

As expected, we're starting to see Alaska activity that should translate to revenues. We're also seeing significant headway on permitting, as well as building relationships with local regulators/influential people, on top of numerous high-profile TV interviews with USAC's CEO. For media people and politicians, the narrative of lacking domestic supply and significant defense needs is very compelling. However, as investors, we must focus on the bottom line.

UAMY has run up so much, trading at a forward "P/E" of 117X with short interest of 12.5%, signaling potential for overvaluation. Even though I was bullish in July, I was admittedly uncomfortable with the massive increase in its price. As I detailed regarding American Battery (ABAT), there's a significant increase in speculation regarding the small strategic materials/tech firms that could benefit from direct government support. UAMY is certainly another example of this, with most of its performance this year driven by hopes and the reality of significant support from the government. Although I often avoid stocks that are too government dependent, that is more reasonable given antimony is so critical to national defense.

Even the best companies can become overvalued as speculative fever is driven to an extreme. At first glance, UAMY's valuation metrics have become alarming. It has around 62 employees, yet is worth nearly $800B, or $12.6M per employee. Its TTM price-to-sales ratio is around 30X, an extremely high figure, with only about $7M in liquidity. See below:

In 2019, it had been struggling to survive for many years as Chinese antimony totally dominated the US market. There were fewer concerns regarding strategic needs to diversify away from China, and China was not so concerned about selling the mineral to its competitors. From my memory, USAC's website was seriously outdated, and its equity was a niche, floundering penny stock.

Today, its website has been redone to update its new CEO's focus on more rapid expansion to renew the American antimony industry. In my view, its CEO, Gary Evan's is an exceptional salesman, often in front of the media, selling this recovery story. But can he deliver? I think we're literally in the weeks where this should become clearer. Though exciting, the reality of executing ambitious mining development plans often brings cost and time overruns, making for "buy the rumor, sell the news" market risk.

The Montana Thompson Falls project is supposed to be completed this year, costing around $15M, with the capacity to produce "over 300 standard tons per month" of antimony trioxide. The current price of antimony trioxide is about $18K/mt, so that could mean up to $5.4M in monthly revenue as production ramps up to meet capacity over 2026.

However, antimony prices can be unclear. In its last 10-Q, it sold at an average price per pound of $28 and a gross profit of $8.47 (costs up 400% YoY). That implies a price per metric ton of about $61K, or nearly $19K in gross profit per ton. At 300 tons, that could mean $5.6M in gross profit per month, or ~$67M annually. The key issue here is extreme volatility not only in antimony prices, but also in USAC's production costs (rising from $3.72 in Q2 2024 to $19.85 per pound in Q2 2025 as its output fell slightly).

The US consumes around 27K mt per year, so even USAC's more immediate target of ~3.6K mt annual production would not result in oversupply, particularly with the national stockpile now down to just ~1.1K mt. This gives USAC room to grow significantly to meet potential demand, but doing so could also lower prices such that its margins compress.

The recent DoD contract is exciting, but it is not precisely "worth $245M." Most media stories focus on the overarching "strategic shortage" narrative, overlooking the details of this deal. Finding that, we can see the contract has a minimum value of $5,000 up to $245M over five years, requiring around 1M pounds, or about 450 mt per year. So realistically, at the seemingly high $60K/mt price, that's probably worth closer to $135M in revenue, or $27M per year.

The Bottom Line

In my opinion, the market has gotten too far ahead of itself with USAC. There is an investing adage, "let your winners run." I've watched UAMY run up for some time, but comparing its five-year revenue and gross profit outlook to its market capitalization, I think it's a bit overvalued, or around the upper end of a reasonable valuation. It's likely reasonable to hold UAMY for investors with a 2030+ horizon, but not for short-term focused investors and speculators. The stock may continue to rally in the short term, but I believe a pullback is more likely.

The analyst consensus looks to its revenue rising to $225M by 2028 with $0.75 in EPS. I do not think that is the most realistic projection. From what I'm seeing, around $60M in annual revenue seems more accurate by 2028, which, based on its typical margins, may bring an EPS of about $0.20. Of course, until we see a significant rise in revenues (expected throughout next year), this projection is speculative. Much of it depends on the very volatile antimony price.

There will also almost certainly be some equity dilution along the way. Its shares outstanding are up around 11% YoY. Luckily, its market cap is so high that it should only take a small amount of sales to raise sufficient capital for its current projects.

My view is downgraded to neutral on valuation concerns and execution risk. We're seeing some signs of successful execution, but to me, not enough to justify its current price point. Although the Federal government seems keen on accelerating the permitting process, that does not eliminate regulatory barriers and the need for local partnerships. Much of that is beyond USAC's control, and regulators are almost always slower than companies.

To me, all the hype and speculation surrounding its expansion has pushed the stock up a bit beyond its feasible profit growth potential. As I'm estimating an EPS of $0.20 around 2028, with strong growth potential thereafter, I value it at 20X that figure, or $4. Keep in mind, it was just $2.8 when I covered it two months back, so I think that's a realistic price target. Still, I concede that my target is very speculative, based on the relatively opaque profitability and supply/demand dynamics of the antimony market.

data.title: VFLO: Quality, Value, And Growth In One Basket
data.description:
Introduction To The VictoryShares Free Cash Flow ETF

The VictoryShares Free Cash Flow ETF (NASDAQ:VFLO), which has been in existence since June 21, 2023, is backed by Victory Capital- the Texas based global asset management firm. VFLO, which has accumulated AUM of close to $5B in a little over two years, is priced at an expense ratio of 0.39%, and offers an annual yield of 1.45% (VFLO’s distributions are paid out every month) at current prices.

What Does the VFLO ETF Do?

Put simply, VFLO focuses on a basket of 50 US stocks which are known for their relatively high FCF [free cash flow] yields, relatively cheap valuations, and relatively high expected growth rates (on the topline, EBITDA and EPS).

Now, some of you may be wondering what an FCF yield is? Well let’s break that up into two parts- the numerator and the denominator. First you have the FCF or free cash flow (the numerator) which measures the excess cash that a company has left, after accounting for its operating expenses and capital expenses.

In many quarters, this metric is construed to be the epitome of a quality business, as it shows that the company is adroit enough to not only generate ample revenue, but also keep a lid on its costs, and then have sufficient ammunition to either return that excess cash to shareholders (via dividends or share buybacks), grow the business by engaging in mergers & acquisitions, or perhaps reinvest it back into the business through higher research & development. Companies can always make mistakes in how they deploy that free cash (which then becomes a capital allocation problem), but the ability to consistently generate ample free cash year in year out, is a difficult skill that not all of them can manage.

The second part of the FCF yield equation, or the denominator, typically focuses on the market-cap of a stock, but in VFLO’s case they also take into consideration the net debt (long-term debt, and short-term debt, minus the idle cash on the balance sheet), which in essence translates to the enterprise value of a stock. Measuring the free cash flow relative to the total enterprise value is seen as a more comprehensive gauge of how much FCF all investors in VFLO can expect (and not just shareholders).

VFLO doesn’t just randomly pickup up high FCF yielders from the US markets, but goes about its business by attempting to replicate the performance of an index called the Victory U.S. Large Cap Free Cash Flow Index [VULFI], which in turn is created by VettaFi LLC (a fintech subsidiary of the TMX Group Limited) using a rules-based methodology.

VULFI is a subset of the VettaFi US Equity Large/Mid-Cap 1000 Index [VUEL1I] and in the first step of its construction all financial and REIT stocks are eliminated from consideration. This makes sense as the business models of financial businesses and REITs aren’t incentivized to maintain large amounts of free cash. More so, the accounting impact, particularly with regards to depreciation, can end up providing a misleading picture on the FCF of REITs when one should really be focusing on rental income.

Nonetheless, after this, the largest 400 profitable stocks are chosen even as companies with negative expected FCF and earnings are also weeded out.

What is “expected FCF”? Well, this is a combination of not just the trailing FCF (over the past twelve months) that has been generated by a business, but also the expected FCF (over the next twelve months), which is based on consensus estimates.

In the next stage, the top 75 stocks based on expected FCF yield are chosen, after which this pool is brought down to the final 50 stocks based on the highest growth scores (the average of long-term sales, EBITDA and EPS estimates). The goal here is to weed out stocks which may have high FCF generating ability, but low growth prospects. The final 50 stocks are then weighted on the basis of not just the total FCF, but also the FCF yield. Basically, stocks with a higher quantum of FCF and higher FCF yield, receive the largest weights in this portfolio (although individual security exposure is capped at just 4% so one doesn’t have to worry about any major concentration effects).

What Are The Defining Traits Of VFLO’s Portfolio?

As noted earlier, the base universe of VFLO’s portfolio is the VettaFi US Equity Large/Mid-Cap 1000 Index [VUEL1I], and the name clearly suggests that this is a large and mid-cap-based index. As a result, note that VFLO’s small-cap exposure is very minimal (less than 2%) with a heavy tilt towards mid-caps (59% of the portfolio). Some of you may wonder why there is even a 2% exposure. Well, while VFLO aims to replicate VULFI, typically only 80% of its net assets are based on the latter. Also between rebalancing dates (which takes place only four times per year), there is the prospect of some stocks transitioning from the mid-cap to small-cap bracket.

However, given that VFLO weighs its stocks on the basis of quantum of free cash flow (besides the FCF yield), we are surprised to not see a higher proportion of giant-cap stocks. Basically, almost two-thirds of this portfolio consists of large and mid-caps.

From a stylistic angle, large and mid-cap “value” stocks dominate the holdings accounting for close to two-thirds of the portfolio. These are stocks that are typically characterized by low price-to-earnings ratios, low price-to-book value ratios, high dividend payouts, etc.

From a sector perspective, we already know that VFLO doesn’t dabble with financials or REITs, so there is the chance of some strong sector exposure towards other sectors. Well, we can see that with two sectors (healthcare and energy) which jointly account for over half the portfolio).

What Are The Risks Associated With VFLO?

For a product that focuses on value stocks and free cash (which serves as the foundation on which large dividend payouts are made), and distributes monthly, VFLO’s dividend yield is quite unappealing (a yield of less than 1.5%, which is over 120bps lower than what the average ETF pays) and thus income investors would do well not to expect the moon.

This could well be on account of it shunning the financial and REIT sectors which are noted for being generous with dividend payouts.

As noted earlier, VFLO’s main exposure is towards the healthcare sector (close to 28% of the portfolio) which remains vulnerable to risks of potential drug price control under the Trump administration going forward. Even if the feasibility of a successful outcome is questionable, discussions around this topic tend to crop up every now and then, triggering unsavory gyrations in the prices of healthcare stocks

Even otherwise its’ worth noting that healthcare stocks aren’t necessarily staring at the most resplendent earnings outlook through FY26. Next year healthcare stocks’ earnings will likely grow at a pace (just 10%) that is lower than most other sectors and the S&P 500 as a whole (14%)

Given the elaborate construction and weighting surrounding not just the trailing FCF, but also forward looking FCF (which is subject to constant changes by the sell-side analyst community which provides the consensus estimates), and VFLO’s individual stock capping of 4%, this portfolio will be prone to constant churn and won’t appeal to those who like to see stability in their portfolios. For context, last year, the annual turnover ratio worked out to a whopping 118% (most ETFs see annual churn of less than 30%), which suggests that a lot of these holdings that VFLO holds aren’t even held for close to a year. With such a high level of annual churn, VFLO is also susceptible to higher transaction costs that could weigh on the overall returns of the fund.

VFLO’s portfolio construction (based on expected FCF) also involves some element of placing faith in forward looking estimates by the sell-side analyst community. These estimates are not always reliable and could end up providing a false picture of things.

Who Is VFLO For?

We view VFLO as suitable for investors who assign credence to the quality factor, stocks with relatively cheap valuations, and decent growth potential, while also desiring a recurring monthly distribution (although this isn’t hugely lucrative, as we’ll touch upon later)

FCF is widely perceived to be an apt barometer of high quality, and its fair to say that the market certainly recognizes that. For the sake of further clarity, note that historically (over a span of 33 years), US stocks who belonged to the lowest quintile from the trailing FCF yield grossly underperformed the S&P 500 (on an annualized basis), whereas those in the highest quintile, outperformed the S&P 500 by over 560bps! In addition to that stocks which score highly on the “expected” FCF yield front, which is what VFLO is oriented towards (where even forward FCF prospects are considered) performed even better.

Then we noted earlier how VFLO tilts toward value style stocks. Well, do consider that VFLO’s portfolio is priced at very compelling valuations, not just on a price-to-cash flow basis (a multiple of less than 8x which translates to a 45% discount over the S&P 500 and the Russell 1000) but also on a price-to-earnings basis (a multiple of ~13x, which is also 45% cheaper than the two benchmarks).

Even though at the heart of it, VFLO is more of a value play, its portfolio construction also involves eradicating low growth (in terms of sales, EBITDA, and EPS) FCF yielders, which also makes it suitable for those who want stocks with respectable growth profiles.

What Are Some Of The Alternatives To FLOW?

Two close peers of VFLO that also construct portfolios on the basis of the FCF yield are the Global X U.S. Cash Flow Kings 100 ETF (FLOW), and the Pacer US Cash Cows 100 ETF (COWZ).

The latter is the most established and popular product in this space with a listing history of close to nine years, AUM of almost $20B, and the tightest bid-ask spreads of just 0.02% (reflecting the liquidity seen in this ticker. It also covers the widest portfolio of stocks (102) and tilts mainly to large-caps unlike the other two. COWZ also tilts towards the enticing technology sector. However, note that COWZ is the only one of the three that pays dividends on a quarterly basis, and it also comes across as the least cost efficient (an expense ratio of 0.49%). The annual churn of over 150% is also quite concerning.

FLOW appears to be a very low-profile product with less than $25M in AUM and wide bid-ask spreads of 0.19% (it is also the youngest of the three products). However, it deserves some credit for being the most cost efficient, the most lucrative from a yield perspective (like VFLO, it too pays monthly) and also witnesses the lowest degree of churn (although still high by general ETF standards). FLOW also stands out for tilting towards consumer cyclical stocks unlike the other two.

Two areas where VFLO stands out from these alternatives is that it is the only one which also incorporates a forward-looking screening of expected FCF (the other two base the portfolio on trailing FCF), and it also doesn’t take into account REIT stocks.

Summary

VFLO, a narrow portfolio (50 stocks) built around the thesis of expected free cash flow, offers a rare blend of quality, value and growth stocks while also incorporating a useful forward-looking viewpoint and a monthly distribution schedule. While its elaborate construction and sizeable churn may put off investors who appreciate stability, studies have shown that stocks that score highly on the basis of expected FCF, tend to outperform the rest of the pack.

This article answers these three main questions about VFLO:

  • How does VFLO build its portfolio of stocks?
  • Who is VFLO meant for and what are the risks they could face?
  • How does VFLO compare to other funds that use FCF in the selection process?

Editor's Note: This article is intended to provide a general overview of the ETF for educational purposes only and, unlike other articles on Seeking Alpha, does not offer an investment opinion about the ETF.

data.title: D-Wave Quantum: The Only Quantum You Can Use Today, But I Would Still Sell
data.description:
Introduction

In my previous breakdown of the quantum computing industry, I argued that the “Holy Grail” general-purpose quantum computer (the one that could crack every encryption and simulate molecules at a scale unimaginable for today’s machines) is still years, maybe decades away. I recommend you read that piece first if you are interested in the industry, because I tried to go deep into how it all works and why we may never see this stronger-than-all computer. In this article, I will talk about annealing and the gate model. The previous piece described the gate model in detail, while this one will focus on annealing.

D-Wave Quantum Inc. (NYSE:QBTS) is the only public pure-play quantum company that is actually generating revenue today from its customers. But it’s not building the type of quantum computers most people imagine when they hear the word “quantum.” Instead, it focuses on a very different approach (quantum annealing), which is something entirely different from what I explained yesterday. It has different technology, commercial potential, and as a result, the stock will behave slightly differently.

Annealing vs. Gate Model

Most of the names I discussed in my previous piece, like IBM (IBM), Google (GOOGL), IonQ (IONQ), and Quantinuum (HON), are focused on building gate-model quantum computers. These are the machines that, once error-corrected (something I called logical qubits in the last article) and scaled to hundreds of thousands of qubits, could revolutionize cryptography, chemistry, and countless other fields. They are basically building that imaginary quantum computer that will be stronger than all of the computers in the world combined and will be hacking into every encryption. But there is a tiny problem. Nobody is even remotely close to that, and there are no guarantees someone ever will be. It’s not that we are years away. If some major technological or scientific breakthrough doesn’t happen soon, we could be decades away.

D-Wave, however, uses quantum annealing, which is entirely different, and this is the reason it can start commercial operations already today. It is quantum-powered, but it’s not what you initially imagine when you hear quantum computer, and it never will be. The whole concept is much simpler: you start with qubits in a kind of “fluid” state, and instead of manipulating them step by step like in the gate model (manipulating them is the biggest challenge), you let the system naturally settle into the lowest-energy configuration.

That lowest-energy state often represents the best solution to an optimization problem. It could solve a problem of routing trucks across a city or scheduling thousands of workers across shifts. In general, it’s problems where there are millions of possible combinations, but one or two that are more efficient.

So D-Wave isn’t trying to solve all of quantum’s promises. Its focus is on optimization, logistics, some machine learning applications, and simulations. Management stopped at the beginning of the latest call to insist that annealing isn’t a niche approach, and on this point, they’re right. But there were a couple of points where their explanation is stretched. In general, I wouldn’t take a word from an interested party for the whole truth, but it relates in general to all public companies and their managers. Let’s see the quote first, and then discuss it:

Before turning to commercial updates, I wanted to take a moment to remind everyone of the differences between annealing and gate model, as there still appears to be continued misinformation that we believe is confusing the market.

Annealing and gate model are different types of quantum computing approaches that solve different types of problems. According to peer-reviewed research, gate model quantum computers will not offer advantages for all problem types. Multiple research results have shown that annealing quantum computers outperform and are expected to continue to outperform gate model on optimization problems. So gate model cannot universally solve all problems better than classical.

It is also important to understand that annealing is not a niche solution. We believe that annealing quantum computing is well suited to a broad set of problems, including AI machine learning, quantum simulation and business optimization, which is ubiquitous in today's modern enterprise. Business optimization problems encompass things like workforce scheduling, production scheduling, resource allocation, vehicle routing and so on.

Together, these represent extensive use cases with far-reaching potential. To characterize annealing quantum computing as niche is misleading and ill-informed. Both annealing and gate model quantum computers can solve a broad range of problems and each has its limitations with problems it cannot efficiently solve.

Annealing quantum computing is very good at solving optimization problems, which cannot be efficiently solved by gate model. Gate model quantum computers, once commercialized, are expected to be very good at quantum chemistry and 3D fluid dynamics, which cannot be efficiently solved by annealing. And both systems, both systems can tackle linear algebra and factorization, meaning problems related to AI, machine learning and cryptography. We believe that organizations will need both annealing and gate model systems in order to address their full problem sets. This is why D-Wave is building both types of quantum computers for our customers.

What the management is right about is that it’s not really “niche.” The optimization is everywhere, and it’s crucial for many huge, global industries. The idea that annealing could become the specialized tool that businesses use makes sense. If gate-model quantum computers eventually arrive at scale, they could replace the annealing, but it’s very early to talk about it. There will be many important details, like scaling, cost-effectiveness, use cases, etc.

Where I think management stretches the story is in how fast this coexistence becomes commercially meaningful. They’re right that optimization problems are everywhere, but the difference between quantum computers being theoretically better at this than regular computers, and someone actually ready to pay hundreds of millions for it, is huge. One of the major concerns in the quantum industry is whether it will be cost-effective.

What is interesting is that management, while protecting annealing, said they are working on both. So it means they recognize the need to work on the gate model. What management did not say is that their efforts on this are far behind competitors like IBM, Google, and Quantinuum. It’s a big and very expensive problem to solve. I described it in the previous piece.

A Working Product in the Market

But short term, there are many advantages to what D-Wave is doing. Unlike IonQ or Rigetti (RGTI), which are still mostly research-driven with cloud demos, D-Wave’s Advantage2 system is already commercially available. Customers can buy access through the cloud or even install on-premise systems, as the Julich Supercomputing Center in Germany has done.

Advantage2 has shown meaningful technical progress over the previous generation, with double coherence times, 40% more energy scale, and higher connectivity between qubits. D-Wave claims to have achieved a kind of “quantum supremacy” over regular computers. Whether D-Wave’s claims of supremacy will stand the test of time is still up for debate, but the direction looks good. They have customers like E.ON, GE Vernova, Nikon, and NTT Data, and even academic and government partners like the U.K.’s National Quantum Computing Center and the University of Oxford. It has also built proofs of concept in aerospace, where a Fortune 500 client is testing a dozen optimization cases, and even in law enforcement, where North Wales Police used D-Wave’s system to optimize patrol car deployment.

Financial Results

In the latest quarter, D-Wave reported $3.1 million in revenue, up 42% year over year. Bookings reached $1.3 million, almost double the prior year’s quarter, and gross margins is pretty high at over 60%.

GAAP gross margin for the second quarter was 63.8%, representing a slight improvement from the second quarter of fiscal 2024 GAAP gross margin of 63.6%. Non-GAAP gross margin for the second quarter was 71.8%, a slight decrease of 1.3% from the second quarter of fiscal 2024 non-GAAP gross margin of 73.1%.

But the losses are much bigger. On a GAAP basis, the company has a net loss of $167 million, though most of that came from warrant revaluation. On an adjusted basis, the loss was closer to $25 million, which still reflects the heavy cost of R&D and scaling. Adjusted EBITDA loss was $20 million for the quarter.

Net loss for the second quarter was $167.3 million or $0.55 per share, an increase of $149.5 million or $0.45 per share from the second quarter of fiscal 2024 net loss of $17.8 million or $0.10 a share. The increase in the net loss was primarily due to $142 million in noncash nonoperating charges related to the remeasurement of the company's warrant liability as well as realized losses stemming from actual warrant exercises.

The one bright spot is the balance sheet, but investors won’t really like that. D-Wave now holds over $800 million in cash, its strongest liquidity position ever. That came at the cost of heavy dilution. Shares outstanding have increased significantly from around 127 million at the end of 2022 to over 340 million today. Investors who bought earlier have already paid for this liquidity.

Valuation is extraordinary to put lightly. With a market cap of nearly $9.5 billion and revenues barely over $15 million for the first half of 2025, D-Wave trades at over 350x forward sales. For context, that’s more expensive than all of the Nvidia’s, Palantirs, and whatever expensive stock you can think of during the AI hype. This stock is not valued like a confident player it could become in optimization, but like a company that created the first quantum computer and took over the world with it. In the past. And now it’s just owns everything.

Jokes aside, I don’t think investors understand what they are actually buying if they drive this stock to a high valuation like this. And I don’t blame them. Quantum computing is this magical unicorn no one understands, but also almost no one is trying to. I have read so many articles and valuations of different quantum stocks and the sector as a whole, but I realized people are not talking about what we really invest in. There is no discussion of whether what they are waiting for is really possible to achieve. It doesn’t, by the way, mean I did not like D-Wave. It’s an interesting company. But with their roadmap as of today, I don’t think they would be fairly valued at this level if they have already achieved everything planned for the next few years.

Future Possibilities

The bullish story around D-Wave really comes down to a few things. First, they have a commercial head start. No one has that really yet. D-Wave is already selling systems and offering cloud access to customers today.

Second, the management has said that they are also working on a gate model. I am not sure if it's that great of idea given how far they are behind, but if they pull that off together with the annealing, they could create a very unusual position in the sector. And third, the U.S. government, Japan, South Korea, and parts of Europe have already opened the door to partnerships. Defense, logistics, and energy optimization are exactly the kind of problems annealing is well-suited to, and if those institutions decide this technology is worth backing, D-Wave could benefit disproportionately.

But there’s a bearish side as well. Annealing may turn out to be too narrow in scope. Or not cost-effective. If gate-model systems begin scaling, they could replace annealing altogether. And even if both approaches do coexist, D-Wave is years behind in gate-model, competing against giants like IBM, Google, and Microsoft with their huge R&D budgets.

The company survives by selling more stock, and that comes at the expense of existing shareholders. Finally, the valuation is already so high as if D-Wave will dominate optimization and stay relevant in gate-model at the same time.

Conclusion

D-Wave is not “that” quantum computer. Not the one that will crack RSA encryption or model new drugs. But it is a quantum company (not a computer) with a product on the market today.

For investors, the trade-off is clear. On the one hand, there is real technology, real customers, and a lot of cash. On the other hand, there is a $9.5 billion market cap against tiny revenues, intense dilution, and the huge risk that annealing turns out to be a technological dead-end.

If you’re bullish, well, I think you are very brave to buy it now. If you’re bearish, you see this speculative bubble priced decades ahead of its time. For me, it’s a fascinating company to follow, a pioneer in proving that quantum can deliver value today, but at these valuations, it’s a lottery ticket rather than an investment.

Note: I never short stocks, and I wouldn’t start with something that’s already up more than 2,000% in a year. My Sell rating simply means that if I had been holding it from earlier, I would be taking profits now.

data.title: India's Hidden Reform: Why U.S. Investors May Be Missing The Real Story
data.description:
By Christopher Gannatti, CFA

The Headline Problem

If you're a U.S. investor paying attention to India in September 2025, chances are you've seen only one storyline: geopolitics. India buys discounted oil from Russia. Prime Minister Modi is seen shaking hands with Vladimir Putin and Xi Jinping. There's discomfort in Washington about whether India is truly a partner or just playing all sides.1

This is the dominant narrative, reinforced by soundbites, often with a skeptical undertone. For many investors, it breeds hesitation. If the world is fracturing into blocs, why take exposure in a country that appears "non-aligned"?

But focusing on this alone risks missing the far bigger story. Because while U.S. audiences hear about geopolitics, what they're not hearing about is something far more consequential for investors: India's Next-Gen GST Reform.2

The Missed Reform Story

Ahead of the Diwali festival in late October 2025, the Indian government will roll out sweeping reforms to its Goods and Services Tax (GST), the country's unified consumption tax, akin in scope to a national value-added tax (VAT).

The changes weren't subtle. Figure 1 is a summary document prepared by India's government to detail the high-level changes.

Figure 1: India's Next-Gen GST Reform

Source: Government of India, Central Board of Indirect Taxes and Customs, "Next-Gen GST reform: Historic Diwali gift for the nation" [infographic], CBIC, 2025. Ghee is a type of clarified butter. Namkeens, Bhujia & Mixtures is a broad category of traditional Indian salty snacks. LPG is liquified petroleum gas. CNG is compressed natural gas. cc stands for cubic centimeter. mm stands for millimeters. LED stands for light-emitting diode. LCD stands for liquid crystal display. 2.5 Lakh rupees per month is 250,000 rupees, and is roughly similar in value to $3,000, as of September 5, 2025. MSMEs stands for micro, small and medium enterprises.

Looking just at the six headers in figure 1, the government's intent comes into sharper focus. Three of the categories—Daily Essentials, Farmers & Agriculture and Healthcare—are all being pushed toward a 5% GST rate. That signals a deliberate policy choice to lower the cost of necessities for households, farmers and patients. These are the foundations of day-to-day life: food, hygiene, farming inputs and medical care. By making them cheaper, the government is clearly prioritizing broad affordability, household consumption and support for small producers and rural communities.

At the same time, two other categories, Automobiles and Electronic Appliances, are being normalized to an 18% rate, down from the punitive 28% that had long discouraged demand. This creates breathing room for middle-class consumers and could accelerate spending on durable goods. The sixth category, Affordable Education, goes even further, dropping rates to nil. That underscores education as a public good rather than a taxable luxury, placing it on par with zero-rated essentials. Taken together, the six headers show a government that is easing pressure on both the household wallet and the engines of small enterprise, while also giving a symbolic and material boost to human capital investment through free-of-tax education.

India's Real Story Isn't Geopolitics; It's the Consumption Revival

The GST reform immediately expands household spending power. Roughly 99% of goods that are currently taxed at 12% are moving to 5%, and about 90% of goods currently taxed at 28% are moving to 18%.3 That means essentials, health care, educational items and even aspirational durables become significantly cheaper for 1.4 billion people. In a country where ~60% of GDP is consumption-led, this is not a marginal adjustment; it is a direct injection of demand into the system.4 First-order effects show up in household wallets, rural affordability for tractors and farm inputs and urban budgets freed up for consumer goods and services.

The second-order effects are already visible in earnings momentum and market positioning. Consumer staples, which had suffered two years of downgrades, just posted their strongest revenue surprises in five quarters. Autos rallied 4% in a single day after the GST announcement, with real estate, metals and staples following closely behind.
